turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has the opinion this morning in case 16-460, Artis v. the District of Columbia. Supplemental jurisdiction statute 28 USC §1367 enables federal district courts to hear state law claims closely related to a plaintiff's federal law claims. When a district court dismisses a plaintiff's federal law claims it ordinarily dismisses as well her state law claims without prejudice to re-filing those claims in state court. This case concerns the time allowed for that re-filing. The governing provision §1367(d) states that the period of limitations for a supplemental state law claim shall be tolled while the claim is pending in federal court plus 30 days after the federal suit is dismissed. The question presented does "shall be tolled" mean that the limitations clock pauses its progression for that time or does the provision mean that the plaintiff has only a 30-day grace period to re-file. In the case before us plaintiff petitioner Stephanie Artis sued her employer the District of Columbia in federal district court alleging a federal employment discrimination claim and three allied claims under DC law which is treated for this purpose as state law. When the district court dismissed Artis' claims two-and-a-half years had passed since she filed her complaint in federal court. Absent any curative provision her state law claims would have been time-barred. 59 days after the federal court dismissal, Artis re-filed her state law claims in the DC Superior Court. That court dismissed them as filed too late. The DC Court of Appeals affirmed holding that §1367(d) gave Artis only a 30-day grace period to re-file in state court. We granted review to resolve the division of opinion amongst state courts on how §1367(d) operates. Statutes that shelter from time bars claims earlier commenced in another forum generally employ one of two means. First the limitations period may be tolled or suspended while the claim is pending elsewhere. The time clock starts running again when the tolling period ends, picking up where it left off. A legislature may instead elect simply to provide a grace period permitting the Statute of Limitations to run while the claim is pending in another forum and averting the risk of a time bar by according the plaintiff a fixed period in which to file. We hold that §1367(d) is not a grace period prescription, instead it stops the clock. The key word in the Statute is "Tolled". In no federal statute of which we are aware has a grace period meaning been ascribed to the word "Tolled". This Court's opinions corroborate that when a limitations period is tolled time ceases to run. Because the DC Court of Appeals ruled otherwise we reverse that court's judgment. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Kennedy, Thomas and Alito have joined.
